title: Capacity for movement is an organisational principle in object representations: EEG data from Experiment 1
description: This dataset contains electroencephalographic (EEG) recordings from 24 participants performing object classification and passive viewing tasks in Experiment 1 of a study investigating how the brain represents object movement and aliveness. Participants classified static images of objects with varying capacities for movement (moving/non-moving) and animacy (alive/not alive) as either alive or capable of movement. The study demonstrates that movement capacity is an important organizational principle in visual object representation, independent of animacy.
methods_description: EEG data were recorded from 24 participants while they completed two tasks: (1) a time-pressured classification task where participants classified single images as alive or not alive (1-second timeout), and (2) a passive viewing task with rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP) streams of images with a color-change detection task. Raw EEG data are provided in BrainVision format (.eeg, .vmrk, .vhdr), with preprocessed data available in EEGlab (.set, .fdt) and cosmoMVPA (.mat) formats.
